past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed at age 22
Major depressive episode at age 27, treated with sertraline for 18 months
Irritable bowel syndrome, diagnosed at age 25
Childhood history of sexual abuse disclosed during prior psychiatric treatment
Migraine without aura, 2-3 episodes per month since adolescence
family_history: Maternal aunt with epilepsy, well-controlled on levetiracetam
Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus
No family history of movement disorders or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.drugs: Denies illicit drug use; denies marijuana
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and 5-year-old daughter in a single-family home
social_history.exercise: Previously active with yoga and running; has not exercised in 3 months due to symptoms
social_history.psychosocial: Reports significant marital conflict over the past 4 months; wife recently disclosed an extramarital affair

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect is flat with occasional tearfulness when discussing his symptoms. Speech is fluent with normal rate and rhythm. No dysarthria. Attention intact, able to spell WORLD backwards. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No evidence of thought disorder.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam normal, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact. During examination, intermittent leftward eye deviation noted that does not follow a consistent pattern and resolves with distraction. No nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in V1-V3 bilaterally. Jaw strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Right upper extremity strength testing shows variable effort with give-way weakness pattern: initial contraction 4/5 in deltoid and biceps, collapsing to 2/5 inconsistently. Left upper extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Bilateral lower extremity strength testing demonstrates 4/5 hip flexors with collapsing quality bilaterally, 5/5 knee extensors, 5/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Hoover sign is equivocal — some involuntary hip extension noted on contralateral hip flexion testing but not consistently reproducible. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to pinprick in the entire right arm in a non-dermatomal distribution, with a sharp midline split. Light touch reportedly diminished over entire right side of body with exact midline demarcation including the trunk. Vibration sense intact at bilateral wrists and malleoli. Proprioception intact at fingers and toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing performed slowly on the right with occasional hesitation but no true dysmetria or intention tremor. Left finger-to-nose normal. Heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements slowed on the right but without true dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient initially refuses to ambulate citing fear of falling. When encouraged, walks with exaggerated sway and lurching pattern, catching himself on examiner and nearby furniture. At one point, nearly falls but demonstrates excellent righting reflexes inconsistent with true ataxia. Tandem gait attempted — demonstrates dramatic lurching but does not fall. When asked to walk backwards, gait paradoxically improves.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had one episode of bilateral upper extremity shaking lasting approximately 90 seconds. He was observed to have asynchronous, variable-amplitude, irregular tremor of both arms with waxing and waning quality. Eyes were forcefully closed during the event. He was responsive to voice throughout, and the tremor briefly stopped when the examiner applied an entrainment maneuver (asking him to tap a rhythm with his foot), then resumed with a different frequency. No cyanosis, tongue biting, or incontinence.

patient.chief_complaint: Episodes of shaking and right-sided weakness
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Marcus Davis is a 29-year-old right-handed African American man who presents to the emergency department after three witnessed episodes of whole-body shaking and progressive right-sided weakness over the past two weeks. The first episode occurred at work approximately two weeks ago while he was treating a patient in the rehabilitation clinic. Colleagues reported that he suddenly stopped speaking, his eyes rolled back, and he developed bilateral upper extremity shaking that lasted about two minutes. He did not fall and was noted to be responsive when his name was called loudly, though he did not speak during the episode. He was taken to an urgent care center where a basic examination and head CT were reportedly normal. He was advised to follow up with neurology. Three days later, he experienced a second, similar episode at home witnessed by his wife, this time associated with subsequent right arm weakness that persisted for several hours before gradually resolving. He was seen in a neurology clinic the following week, where a routine 30-minute EEG was performed and interpreted as normal, though no clinical events were captured during the recording. An outpatient brain MRI was ordered but not yet completed. Over the past five days, he has had three additional episodes of shaking, occurring approximately once daily, each lasting one to three minutes. He reports that the episodes are preceded by a rising sensation in his chest and a feeling of overwhelming anxiety. Between episodes, he endorses persistent weakness in his right arm and leg, intermittent numbness over the entire right side of his body, and difficulty walking due to a sensation of imbalance. He also describes word-finding difficulty that comes and goes. He notes that his symptoms tend to worsen when he is stressed or fatigued and improve somewhat when he is distracted, though he has not explicitly recognized this pattern. He has been unable to work and has been struggling to care for his daughter. His wife brought him to the emergency department today after he had two episodes within six hours, prompting admission for continuous video-EEG monitoring and further evaluation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep spindles and vertex waves in stage 2 sleep

artifacts.note: Intermittent, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No change in background or induction of abnormal activity during 3 minutes of hyperventilation
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal routine EEG. No epileptiform discharges. No focal slowing. Normal background organization with symmetric posterior dominant rhythm. Routine activating procedures did not provoke abnormalities. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: This is a routine 30-minute outpatient EEG. No clinical events were captured during the recording. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended to capture habitual events and assess for EEG correlate.

additional_observations: Normal calvarium and scalp soft tissues
mri.impression: Normal MRI of the brain without and with gadolinium contrast. No evidence of acute infarction, hemorrhage, mass lesion, or demyelinating disease. No structural abnormality to account for the patient's symptoms. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 132 mEq/L (L), Chloride 94 mEq/L (L).

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. Normal intervals. No ST-T wave abnormalities. No evidence of cardiac arrhythmia.

background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep-wake cycling with normal spindles, vertex waves, and K-complexes

artifacts.note: Present during clinical events due to body movement; underlying cerebral activity remains interpretable on referential montage and confirms absence of ictal pattern

output.impression: NORMAL continuous video-EEG over 48 hours. Three habitual clinical events were captured, each demonstrating bilateral asynchronous limb shaking with forced eye closure and preserved responsiveness. No EEG correlate was identified during any event. The background remained normal throughout the recording. No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: functional neurological disorder diagnosis positive signs criteria

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A, et al.
results.summary: FND is diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness) rather than by exclusion of organic disease. Modern reconceptualization emphasizes that FND is a disorder of nervous system functioning, not feigning.

results.title: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures: updated diagnostic criteria and management
results.authors: LaFrance WC Jr, Baker GA, Duncan R, et al.
results.summary: Video-EEG capturing habitual events with no ictal correlate remains the gold standard for PNES diagnosis. ILAE levels of diagnostic certainty: documented (video-EEG captured), clinically established (witnessed by clinician), probable, possible.

results.title: A systematic review of the role of neuroimaging in functional neurological disorder
results.authors: Voon V, Cavanna AE, Cober K, et al.
results.summary: Neuroimaging in FND is typically normal on structural sequences. Functional MRI studies show altered connectivity patterns but are not used clinically for diagnosis.

output.summary: Functional neurological disorder is diagnosed by positive clinical signs rather than by exclusion. Key positive signs include Hoover sign for weakness, tremor entrainment for functional tremor, and video-EEG demonstrating no ictal correlate for psychogenic non-epileptic seizures. The diagnosis requires demonstration of internal inconsistency in the neurological examination.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional weakness

differential.key_features: Episodes have features atypical for epilepsy: asynchronous limb movements, forced eye closure, preserved awareness during events, waxing/waning quality, entrainability. Normal post-event prolactin. Normal routine EEG, though events not yet captured on video-EEG at presentation.

Right-sided weakness and sensory changes could suggest demyelinating disease, but MRI brain is normal without white matter lesions. Non-dermatomal sensory loss with exact midline split is inconsistent with MS. Age and sex are in the typical MS demographic.

Right-sided weakness could suggest left hemispheric stroke, but age 33 without vascular risk factors, normal MRI, fluctuating non-anatomical weakness pattern, and give-way quality argue strongly against vascular etiology.

Young woman with seizure-like episodes and psychiatric symptoms could raise concern for NMDAR encephalitis, but preserved cognition, absence of dyskinesias, normal MRI, and normal EEG background make this less likely. Autoimmune antibody testing should be considered.
Weakness could suggest neuromuscular junction disorder, but weakness pattern is not fatigable, no ptosis or diplopia, give-way pattern not consistent with myasthenic weakness.

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination specifically looking for positive functional signs: Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, distractibility of deficits
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Give-way weakness in right arm, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline splitting, entrainable tremor during episodes, gait inconsistency

optimal_actions.action: Obtain prolonged continuous video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical events captured with no EEG correlate, confirming psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

optimal_actions.action: Review brain MRI to exclude structural lesion
Obtain basic labs including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal; post-event prolactin not elevated

optimal_actions.action: Consider EMG/NCS if peripheral neuropathy or myopathy remains in differential
Consider autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel given age, sex, and seizure-like episodes
Communicate diagnosis to patient using supportive, empathetic approach emphasizing that the symptoms are real but caused by a disorder of nervous system functioning
Refer to psychiatry for evaluation and treatment of comorbid depression, anxiety, and PTSD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification and treatment of psychiatric comorbidity

optimal_actions.action: Refer for cognitive-behavioral therapy or specialized FND physiotherapy

critical_actions: Recognize positive functional signs on examination (give-way weakness, entrainable tremor, non-dermatomal sensory loss)
Obtain prolonged video-EEG to capture events and confirm absence of EEG correlate
Communicate the FND diagnosis to the patient in a supportive, non-dismissive manner
Screen for and address psychiatric comorbidities (depression, anxiety, PTSD)
Avoid unnecessary invasive procedures and iatrogenic harm
Refer for appropriate FND-specific treatment (CBT, specialized physiotherapy)
contraindicated_actions: Starting antiepileptic drugs without EEG evidence of epilepsy
Telling the patient the symptoms are 'all in their head' or 'not real'
Performing unnecessary invasive procedures (brain biopsy, angiogram) without clinical indication
Discharging without a clear diagnosis or follow-up plan
Ordering excessive repeat imaging in the absence of new clinical findings
Prescribing benzodiazepines for the episodes as seizure treatment
key_reasoning_points: The moderate difficulty arises from overlapping features with epilepsy: the patient has a maternal aunt with epilepsy, episodic events with altered responsiveness, and a prior normal EEG without events captured — all features that could initially steer the clinician toward epilepsy
Positive functional signs (give-way weakness, entrainable tremor, midline-splitting sensory loss) are the key to diagnosis — FND should be diagnosed by positive signs, not merely by exclusion
The equivocal Hoover sign adds diagnostic uncertainty appropriate for moderate difficulty — not all functional signs are floridly positive
Normal post-event prolactin supports non-epileptic etiology but is not definitive in isolation

The significant psychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, PTSD from childhood trauma) and acute psychosocial stressors (marital conflict) provide context for the development of functional symptoms
Video-EEG capturing habitual events with no ictal EEG correlate provides the definitive diagnosis of PNES (ILAE documented level of certainty)
The gait examination showing dramatic lurching with excellent righting reflexes and paradoxical improvement walking backwards is a positive functional sign

metadata.difficulty_rationale: Moderate difficulty due to overlapping features with epilepsy, equivocal Hoover sign, family history of epilepsy as potential red herring, and psychiatric comorbidity that could be interpreted as either primary or secondary to neurological disease
